title: See the Milky Way’s Galactic Bulge, Captured in Unprecedented Detail
gist: The ESA's Euclid space telescope captured the most detailed image yet of the Milky Way's galactic bulge, resolving over 60 million individual stars with a field of view 270x larger than Hubble's — a step toward finding the region's exoplanets.

insights.claim: The ESA's Euclid space telescope, parked almost 1 million miles from Earth, has a field of view 270 times larger than Hubble's, enabling it to resolve more than 60 million individual stars within the Milky Way's galactic bulge.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: Wide-field space telescopy is advancing rapidly; relevant to the European space-instrumentation supply chain.
insights.quote: Its field of view is 270 times larger than Hubble’s, which makes it perfect for discerning the more than 60 million stars within the galactic bulge.
insights.locator: Like the Hubble Space Telescope, Euclid

insights.claim: The detailed galactic-bulge image is described as a crucial step in the hunt for exoplanets in the region.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: Exoplanet discovery pipeline keeps expanding the scientific case for next-gen space telescopes.
insights.quote: a crucial step in the hunt for the region’s exoplanets
